data.title: $285 million stolen from Drift: Hackers deliver a fatal blow to DeFi during the bear market.
data.description: Around April 2, 2026, multiple on-chain monitoring and media outlets reported that Drift Protocol, an integrated derivatives and lending protocol within the Solana ecosystem, experienced an abnormal outflow of funds. The project team confirmed that it was under attack, ultimately resulting in the theft of approximately $280 million in funds. The protocol has suspended deposits and withdrawals and is collaborating with security agencies, cross-chain bridges, and trading platforms to address the issue. What is Drift Protocol? Drift is a hybrid DeFi protocol, similar to an exchange: launched in 2021, it started with Solana perpetual contracts and later expanded to spot trading, lending, and more "one-stop-shop" protocol narratives. In 2024, Drift officially stated that the protocol had over $350 million in TVL, more than 175,000 traders, and a cumulative trading volume exceeding $20 billion; in September of the same year, it completed a $25 million Series B, bringing its total funding to $52.5 million. At the mechanism level, Drift documentation explicitly acknowledges its reliance on external oracle accounts and has designed "barriers" including oracle validity, TWAP pruning, price deviation bandwidth verification, and updating market information when necessary to limit specific actions. Its historical narrative has also emphasized that if oracle prices are "invalid or manipulated," it could lead to the depletion of exchange assets in a short period, thus requiring multi-step verification and "multi-range circuit breakers" to create a reaction window. However, this attack demonstrates that even if a protocol has a relatively robust "market risk control barrier," as long as attackers can access or influence the "permission layer" (administrator keys, multi-signature, and risk parameter governance channels), they can potentially turn the barrier itself into a tool that can be misused—for example, by distorting certain thresholds or raising the collateral weight of a certain asset to an irrational level, ultimately allowing the system to "legally" execute asset transfers under the premise that "rules have been rewritten." Drift Protocol Responds to $280 Million Loss: Social Engineering and Durable Nonce Attack Drift Protocol issued a statement regarding today's security incident, stating that a malicious actor gained unauthorized access to the protocol and quickly took over management of the Drift Security Committee using a new attack method involving durable nonces. Drift stated that this was a highly sophisticated attack, seemingly planned and executed in stages over several weeks, including techniques such as pre-signing transactions using durable nonce accounts and delaying execution. According to Drift's current investigation, the incident was not caused by vulnerabilities in Drift programs or smart contracts, and there is no evidence that related mnemonic phrases were leaked. Drift believes the attackers obtained unauthorized or forged transaction approvals before execution, and the durable nonce mechanism and sophisticated social engineering likely played a key role. This incident resulted in the transfer of approximately $280 million in assets from the protocol. Drift stated that the attackers were able to complete the attack through several steps: first, pre-deploying access paths through a durable nonce account; then obtaining sufficient approval authority in multisignature, specifically 2/5 multisignature approval; next, executing a malicious administrator privilege transfer within minutes to gain protocol-level access control; and finally, using this authority to introduce malicious assets and remove all existing withdrawal restrictions, thereby attacking existing funds. Currently, all funds deposited into the lending module, vault, and trading accounts have been affected. Unaffected assets include: DSOL not deposited into Drift, including assets staked to Drift Validators; and insurance fund assets, which will be withdrawn from the protocol and transferred to a more secure environment for protection. As a precaution, Drift has frozen all remaining functionality of the protocol and updated its multisignature configuration, removing affected wallets. This incident has spilled over to multiple DeFi protocols within the Solana ecosystem. Projects such as Reflect Money, Ranger Finance, Neutral Trade, Elemental DeFi, Project 0, Lulo Finance, Asgard Finance, DeFi Carrot, Pyra, xPlace, and Fuse Wallet have been confirmed to be affected, with some projects suspending minting, redemption, or deposit/withdrawal functions. Ranger Finance stated that it faces approximately $900,000 in risk exposure, representing about 6% of its TVL; Pyra indicated that it has suspended related card functionality due to user funds being affected by Drift earnings. Technical Analysis: Fake CVT Tokens and Oracle Manipulation According to previous analysis by Helius developer Ichigo, the Drift Protocol incident may be related to attackers constructing fake CVT tokens and manipulating the Switchboard oracle, thus forming an attack path. He stated that the attackers subsequently used social engineering to enter the security committee governance process and, despite the potential impairment of multi-signature permissions, pushed the token to be listed with high weight as collateral. The attackers minted "fake tokens" weeks in advance and used a pool with extremely low liquidity (approximately $500) on Raydium as a "pricing anchor," then continuously created price and transaction traces through wash trading, paving the way for subsequent oracle price history. It's worth noting that the attackers deposited approximately 20 million CVT tokens with near-zero value, which, after being overvalued to over $100 million, were used as collateral to borrow real assets within the protocol and transfer funds. The total scale is estimated to exceed $200 million. Following the attack on Drift Protocol, its governance token, DRIFT, plummeted by over 40% in the past 24 hours. The annualized negative fee rate for DRIFT U-margined perpetual contracts on major exchanges like Binance has soared to the maximum, exceeding 6,000%, resulting in a massive subsidy from short sellers to long sellers. Timeline of the Incident On March 23, the attackers completed the initial nonce setup. A total of four durable nonce accounts were created that day, two of which were associated with multisignature members of the Drift Security Committee, and the other two were controlled by the attackers. Drift believes this means that at least two-fifths of multisignature signers had signed transactions associated with durable nonce accounts, thus enabling delayed execution. March 25: The suspected attacker's main wallet address (HkGz4KmoZ7Zmk7HN6ndJ31UJ1qZ2qgwQxgVqQwovpZES) obtained initial funds through Near Intents, remained inactive for a long period, and then suddenly activated and received a large amount of funds from the Drift vault on the day of the attack. March 27: Due to a change in security committee members, Drift performed a planned multisignature migration of its security committee. March 30: New durable nonce activity reappeared. A new durable nonce account was created for a member in the updated multisignature. Drift believes this indicates the attacker has once again gained actual usable permissions for 2/5 of the signers in the updated multisignature. On April 1st, the attack entered the execution phase. First, Drift executed a test withdrawal transaction from the insurance fund. Approximately one minute later, the attacker swiftly executed two pre-signed durable nonce transactions, separated by only four slots. The first transaction created and approved a malicious administrator transfer, while the second transaction approved and executed that transfer. At this point, the attacker officially took over key permissions of the protocol. On the afternoon and evening of April 1st, the on-chain monitoring tool MLM detected abnormal fund flows in related addresses, totaling approximately $270.6 million (about 50% of Drift TVL), mainly involving assets such as JLP and USDC. Helius CEO Mert stated that on-chain signs indicated the protocol may have been attacked; Drift subsequently issued a statement confirming that it was monitoring abnormal activity in the protocol and advised users not to deposit funds into the protocol until further notice. Drift stated that the attack was achieved through a combination of two factors: first, a pre-signed durable nonce transaction, allowing the attacker to delay execution at a future point in time; and second, the approval of multiple multi-signature signers was compromised, which was likely achieved through targeted social engineering attacks or transaction information spoofing. Industry Expert Opinions and Governance Reflections Ledger CTO Charles Guillemet stated that the attack was not a smart contract vulnerability, but rather a long-term, latent breach of the multi-signature mechanism. Hackers allegedly gained control of the multi-signature holder's device or private key, misleading operators into approving malicious transactions. This method is highly similar to the Bybit incident last year, which was suspected to be linked to the North Korean hacking group (DPRK). He called on the industry to improve endpoint detection capabilities and adopt hardware-supported plaintext signatures to prevent operational risks. Uniswap founder Hayden Adams bluntly stated that centralized projects must stop calling themselves DeFi; if an administrator's key can drain all funds, it is essentially CeFi. Chaos Labs founder Omer Goldberg added that the Drift protocol's signing keys have complete control over market creation, oracle allocation, and withdrawal limits, and lack time locks, allowing attackers to allegedly steal funds in just about 10 seconds. On-chain tracking shows that the suspected attacker's address (HkGz4KmoZ7Zmk7HN6ndJ31UJ1qZ2qgwQxgVqQwovpZES) quickly transferred/exchanged funds after the attack and then transferred them to Ethereum via Wormhole cross-chain transfer. When some USDC was transferred through the Circle CCTP bridge, Circle failed to freeze the funds in a timely manner, drawing criticism from Delphi Digital co-founder Tommy Shaughnessy and on-chain detective ZachXBT, who argued that Circle was slow to react despite having centralized freezing capabilities. Currently, Drift is collaborating with several security companies to investigate the root cause of the incident and is also working with cross-chain bridges, exchanges, and law enforcement agencies to track and freeze the stolen assets. Drift stated that it will release a more detailed post-incident analysis report in the coming days and welcomes any information or leads related to the investigation.

data.title: Anxiety Economy: Why Transactions Are Exploding
data.description:
Author: Zheng Jie Source: artemis Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Summary

  • In short, an entire generation has calculated the returns of passive investing and found that they are far from sufficient. Financial anxiety will replace greed as the core driving force of trading.

  • The interval between forming market judgments and executing trades is approaching zero.

  • AI will narrow the information gap between retail investors and institutions, but the returns of ordinary active traders will continue to underperform the market.

  • AI will narrow the information gap between retail investors and institutions, but the returns of ordinary active traders will continue to underperform the market.

  • Winners: Robinhood, Coinbase, IBKR Losers: Traditional brokerages selling patience to a speed-driven generation I. The math is clear to everyone Saving $1200 per month, with an 8% annual return, for 24 years. This is the standard answer for someone starting from scratch at age 26 to earn $1 million—by then you'll be 50. You earn $90,000 a year, but after taxes, New York rent, and student loans, you can only actually save $1200 per month. Investing in index funds with an annualized return of 8%: Approximately $220,000 in 10 years; approximately $710,000 in 20 years. Even with a smooth and uneventful investment, one might only reach a million dollars by age 50. This is the baseline scenario assuming everything is done correctly.

    Then you open your phone: your college roommate cashed out $800,000 in startup stock options; your cousin's house bought in 2019 appreciated by 60%; a 24-year-old stranger turned $10,000 of meme coins into $400,000 in just a few weeks. You know these are extreme cases, most people won't post about their losses, but it doesn't matter, you still feel like you're being left far behind.

    So, you start trading.

    ... This is the anxiety economy: an era where financial anxiety becomes the core source of demand for an entire category of products. It serves those who have calculated the costs and benefits and realized that a conservative approach simply cannot achieve their goals. The industry generally believes that the growth in transaction volume stems from: better products, lower fees, more assets, the widespread adoption of AI tools, and improved user experience. These are all true, but not the whole story. The deeper driving force is emotion: the entire industry is serving the same feeling: I'm falling behind, I need a faster way to catch up.

    II. Data Doesn't Lie

    Since 2000:

    • S&P 500 return approximately 400%

    • Home prices have risen approximately 230%

    • Median US wages have only increased by approximately 110%

    IV. Industry Landscape

    The seemingly crowded trading market can actually be clearly divided into four layers:

    1. Discovery Layer: Wallets, deposit channels, data products, and AI tools. Users view prices, receive alerts, deposit funds, and form trading intentions here. Brokerages do not control this layer—you already know what you want to do before you open Robinhood.

    2. Brokerage Layer: Brokerages and Crypto Super Apps.

    3. Brokerage Layer: Brokerages and Crypto Super Apps.

    4. Brokerage Layer: Brokerages and Crypto Super Apps.

      5. Intent translates into action here: click to buy, set up a position, confirm the order. The platform strives to control user funds and transactions; the more funds, the harder it is for users to leave. Trading Venue Layer: Exchanges, perpetual contract platforms, aggregators, prediction markets, tokenized exchanges. Transactions are truly settled here. Globalized assets and 24/7 trading will allow this layer to capture more traffic. Infrastructure Layer: Liquidity, clearing systems, blockchain, tokenized infrastructure. Furthest from users, unconcerned with app wars, only profiting from trading volume. The biggest winners will be: the apps users first open, the platforms that manage funds, and the venues for executing trades. V. Value Flow The ultimate winners will be those companies that enable users to most easily translate this feeling into action.

      Winner

      1. Robinhood is the most obvious winner. From day one, it has served those who "feel behind": zero commission, fractional shares, and a simple interface. Now, it adds prediction markets, gold cards, cryptocurrencies, and retirement accounts, boasting the fastest product iteration speed in fintech. In Q1 2026, it launched the AI ​​assistant Cortex, acquired a CFTC-licensed exchange, and reached 3.9 million gold card users, a year-on-year increase of 77%. Its success lies in understanding: trading volume growth is driven by anxiety. 2. Coinbase's perfect synergy between consumer finance and its 24/7 native marketplace. Controlling crypto deposits and underlying infrastructure, USDC becomes the default savings and settlement tool, and the Base chain achieves profitability. Revenue has shifted from fluctuating transaction fees to subscriptions, stablecoin interest, and infrastructure services. Stablecoin revenue reached $332 million in a single quarter, and USDC's market capitalization reached a record high of $76 billion. Even if users stop trading, their funds remain within the system. 3. Interactive Brokers (IBKR) covers over 170 markets in 40 countries, adding over 1 million new accounts in 2025, bringing the total to 4.4 million accounts and $780 billion in client assets. It supports stablecoin deposits, launched a Visa co-branded card, and redesigned its app to incorporate AI market insights. If a company can maintain its professional depth while achieving Robinhood-like simplicity, it will reap the rewards from high-intent users in the anxiety economy. 4. ICE (NYSE's parent company) collects "tolls." The more tradable instruments, the longer the trading period, and the wider the scope of the market, the more commission it takes. In 2025, it achieved record trading volume, with ADV increasing by 14% year-on-year and revenue reaching $9.9 billion. 5. Hyperliquid: A dark horse in the pure trading venue sector. When traditional markets are closed, it becomes the pricing center for assets such as oil. WTI-related contracts see daily trading volume exceeding $2 billion, a more than 100-fold increase in six months. The 24/7 trading venue offers a structural advantage. Traditional brokerages will be the losers. Companies that don't innovate will ultimately be eliminated. If they continue to sell security and long-term planning to a generation driven by speed and hope, they might retain the assets of baby boomers, but they won't win over the next 100 million traders (Generation Z and Alpha). On watch list: Webull has built a powerful product for active traders—offering realistic charting capabilities, longer trading hours, and a convenient mobile experience. However, its risk lies in its mediocre feature positioning: too complex for beginners, and not deep enough for professionals. eToro—one of the earliest platforms to socialize trading. Copy trading, community dynamics, and the ability to follow the ideas of people who know more than you—in today's anxious economic environment, this intuition that "those who understand the market are more favored" only grows stronger. SoFi remains one of the best examples of integrating financial services (banking, lending, credit, and investment) into a single consumer brand. My concern is its market positioning. When people think of SoFi, do they think of "trading" or "student loan refinancing"? Their product range is extensive, but lacks depth. Every company on the list grows with increasing trading volume. Now you should understand why.

      VI. The “False Advantage” Brought by AI

      AI will reshape trading technologically, but its greater impact lies on the psychological level.

      Imagine this: Before the market opens, an AI briefing summarizes your portfolio, identifies key catalysts, and explains the potential impact of the Consumer Price Index, tariffs, or earnings on your holdings. By the time you've finished your coffee, you already have a clear investment strategy. You feel like you have something in your grasp.

data.title: Layoffs, cryptocurrency sales, AI development: MARA's transformation is just a typical microcosm of mining companies' strategies.
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

TL;DR

On April 3, 2026, Bitcoin mining company MARA laid off 15% of its workforce to advance its strategic transformation from a pure Bitcoin mining company to an energy and digital infrastructure company, and to increase its investment in AI infrastructure. The company had previously entered the AI ​​computing power market by acquiring a 64% stake in Exaion. Now, the continued huge losses in its Bitcoin mining business and the explosive growth in demand for AI computing power have become the two core driving forces behind its transformation. Not only MARA, but the AI ​​transformation of mining companies worldwide has already begun…

MARA (NASDAQ:MARA), one of the world's largest Bitcoin mining companies, laid off approximately 15% of its employees, affecting full-time employees and some contract workers across multiple departments. MARA CEO Fred Thiel stated in an internal memo that the layoffs were not purely a financial decision, but rather part of the company's strategic transformation from a pure Bitcoin mining company to an energy and digital infrastructure company.

This move reflects MARA's proactive "slimming down," diverting resources from traditional mining operations and shifting them to the more promising AI field. I. From Mining Company to Digital Infrastructure: MARA's Transformation Path On February 26th of this year, MARA Holdings, Inc. announced a strategic agreement with Starwood Capital Group ("Starwood") and its dedicated data center development platform, Starwood Digital Ventures ("SDV"). This collaboration will help MARA transform and upgrade some of its data centers, building next-generation digital infrastructure to meet the growing needs of enterprise, hyperscale, and AI customers. SDV will lead the design, development, tenant recruitment, construction, and facility operation, while Starwood will provide investment expertise to improve the project's economics. MARA will contribute dedicated, energy-efficient data centers. The two parties will deliver approximately 1 gigawatt of IT capacity, with the potential to eventually reach over 2.5 gigawatts. MARA sits at the intersection of energy and computing, while SDV's development engine provides robust execution and operational capabilities crucial for the transformation and expansion of MARA's intersection into a scalable and sustainable digital infrastructure. The dual-purpose design of these data centers allows them to run AI/enterprise/high-performance computing workloads and Bitcoin mining simultaneously, enabling operational flexibility in an ever-changing market environment. This modular approach allows Marathon to continue its mining operations while securing "highly attractive economic terms" from more profitable data center customers. MARA's AI strategy dates back to 2025. In August 2025, broker HC Wainwright reported that Bitcoin mining company MARA would acquire a 64% stake in Exaion, a high-performance computing (HPC) company under French energy giant EDF, with the potential to increase its stake to 75% by 2027. In February of this year, MARA's official website announced that the acquisition of a 64% stake in Exaion by MARA France had been completed, with EDF remaining a minority shareholder and customer; NJJ acquired a 10% stake in MARA France. Exaion focuses on HPC data centers and security cloud/AI, and its board of directors includes Xavier Niel and MARA CEO Fred Thiel, with plans to accelerate its expansion in Europe. This marks MARA's first substantial entry into the AI/HPC field, transforming from a mining company into a participant in computing power services. II. Why the Transformation? 1. Mining Business Losses Simultaneously with the announcement of the transformation in February, MARA also released its Q4 2025 results: despite some operational improvements, it still incurred substantial losses. In Q4 2025, MARA reported a net loss of $1.7 billion (equivalent to a loss of $4.52 per share), a stark contrast to the $528 million net profit in the same period last year. MARA's fourth-quarter results reflect the severe challenges faced by Bitcoin miners, with multiple headwinds impacting its profitability. The company's financial and operational overview shows that core operating metrics were under pressure overall. Despite a 25% year-over-year increase in hashrate to 66.4 EH/s and a 20% increase in Bitcoin supply to 53,822 BTC, production declined by 19% to 2,011 BTC due to increased network difficulty. MARA successfully improved cost-effectiveness, reducing its daily cost per PET hashrate by 4% to $30.50. However, this was insufficient to offset the impact of Bitcoin price volatility and increased network competition. Adjusted EBITDA plummeted from $796 million in Q4 2024 to negative $1.5 billion, impacted by significant impairments and operational pressures. The company holds approximately $5.3 billion in cash and Bitcoin but faces massive debt of $3.64 billion and has consumed $1.77 billion in leveraged free cash flow over the past 12 months. 2. The Rise of AI MARA's adjustments are also in response to the current trend of the rise of AI. The electricity demand for AI data centers will grow from approximately 50 gigawatts in 2025 to 200 gigawatts in 2030, an increase of 255%, requiring trillions of dollars in capital investment. According to a Goldman Sachs research report, global data center electricity demand will increase by approximately 165%–200% by 2030, with AI-related loads continuing to rise. McKinsey & Company points out that the cumulative investment demand for AI infrastructure (computing power + data centers + electricity) could reach trillions of dollars in the coming years. Under the AI ​​wave, MARA either continues to suffer losses due to the uncertainty of BTC or shifts to the more inelastic computing power market. BTC mining farms are essentially natural AI computing power infrastructure, making MARA's transformation a natural industrial upgrade in line with the trend.

III. Mining Companies Are Collectively Embarking on a Transformation

MARA's transformation is not isolated, but rather a typical microcosm of the entire mining industry.

Over the past year, with the shrinking profit margins of BTC mining and the explosive growth in computing power demand caused by the rise of AI, global mining companies are undergoing a wave of transformation.

According to data released by S&P in February: Although revenue from high-performance computing (HPC) and artificial intelligence (AI) has been relatively limited so far, infrastructure investment is accelerating, and analysts predict that HPC will make a significant revenue contribution from 2026 onwards. HPC is no longer a sideline: for many mining companies, it is expected to become a major pillar of growth in the coming years. In particular, IREN, Terawaulf, and Core Scientific are now almost entirely focused on HPC development, and analysts predict that these businesses will drive most of these companies' revenue growth in 2026.

By 2026, high-performance computing (HPC) revenue will account for 13% of Riot's total revenue.

This shift is even more pronounced for other companies: IREN's HPC revenue is projected to surge from 3% in 2024 to 71% of total revenue; Core Scientific is expected to reach 71% from 5%; HIVE from 7% to 15%; and Cipher Mining and Terawulf are projected to reach 34% and 70% respectively, while their contributions in 2024 will be negligible. This shift highlights the industry's strategic transformation from reliance on cryptocurrencies to growth driven by artificial intelligence and high-performance computing, with miners positioning themselves as high-performance computing infrastructure providers, offering managed services such as power, cooling, and physical infrastructure. The following are case studies of crypto mining company transformation. 1. Core Scientific, Inc. Core Scientific was founded in Seattle in 2017 but later moved its operational headquarters to Austin. Its founders include former Microsoft COO B. Kevin Turner. The company initially focused on Bitcoin mining using renewable energy and digital asset infrastructure. However, due to the plunge in Bitcoin prices and high debt levels, Core Scientific filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in the United States at the end of 2022. Operations continued during the bankruptcy proceedings. In January 2024, after a major restructuring and reorganization, the company emerged from bankruptcy. Since 2024, the company has increasingly focused on high-performance computing (HPC) for artificial intelligence. In 2025, the company signed a $10 billion data center operations contract. In July 2025, CoreWeave announced plans to acquire Core Scientific for $9 billion. In March 2026, Core Scientific announced the sale of approximately $175 million worth of Bitcoin to accelerate the expansion of its AI infrastructure. Meanwhile, Bitcoin mining operations will cease. In addition to the Bitcoin sale, the company also secured a $1 billion loan to build new data centers in multiple U.S. states. As of March 2026, Core Scientific currently operates 10 data centers across seven U.S. states. 2. CoreWeave, Inc. CoreWeave was founded in 2017 in New Jersey by three commodity traders—Michael Intrator, Brian Venturo, and Brannin McBee, along with Peter Salanki. Originally named Atlantic Crypto, it was a cryptocurrency mining company that used graphics processing units (GPUs) to mine Ethereum. Following the cryptocurrency crash of 2018, the company changed its name to CoreWeave in 2019, leveraging its vast GPU inventory to begin providing cloud computing infrastructure to enterprises. With the market's growing demand for artificial intelligence processing in 2022 and 2023, CoreWeave's business, with its exclusive rights to Nvidia GPUs, experienced significant growth. In February 2025, CoreWeave became the first cloud service provider to offer Nvidia GB200 NVL72 chips via cloud computing. IBM announced it would use GB200 clusters to train its Granite AI. In January 2026, CoreWeave received a $2 billion investment from NVIDIA at a price of $87.20 per share, expanding their collaboration to facilitate CoreWeave's data center construction. In February 2026, CoreWeave sought $8.5 billion in new funding, using a large-scale AI infrastructure contract with Meta Platforms as collateral. 3. IREN IREN, formerly known as Iris Energy, was founded in 2018 by brothers Daniel & Will Roberts. The company initially focused on 100% hydroelectric/wind-powered Bitcoin mining, emphasizing "green mining." When it went public in 2021, its hashrate had expanded to 50 EH/s (ranking among the top 5 mining companies globally). During the crypto winter of 2023, it suspended mining expansion and secured access to Texas power grids. It also changed its name to IREN, downplaying its crypto focus. In October 2025, IREN signed a 5-year, $9.7 billion AI cloud service contract with Microsoft. In March 2026, it signed a $3.5 billion contract with Dell to purchase an additional 50,000 NVIDIA Blackwell B300 processors. 4. Terawaulf Founded in 2019, Terawaulf focuses on Bitcoin mining and clean energy. In 2024, it established the subsidiary WULF Compute, specializing in AI/HPC hosting and completely transforming mining farms into liquid-cooled AI data centers. 2025 became a milestone year for its order surge. In August, it signed a 450MW, 10-year, $6.7 billion contract with Fluidstack, backed by Google. In December, it reached a 72.5MW, 10-year, $1.1 billion cooperation agreement with G42/Core42 in the UAE. The year saw a total of 522MW of HPC business signed, with a total contract value of $12.8 billion. AI/HPC revenue reached $16.9 million, accounting for 10% of the total revenue that year. It also secured $3.2 billion in equity and debt investment from Google and a total financing package of $6.5 billion. 5. HIVE HIVE, short for HIVE Digital Technologies Ltd., was founded in 2017 by Frank Holmes, Aydin Kilic, and others. The core team has experience in cryptocurrency, energy, and technology. From its inception, it established a development direction of "clean energy + cryptocurrency mining." HIVE officially launched its AI computing power transformation strategy in 2024. HIVE has become the third largest AI-transformed mining company in North America (after IREN and Terawaulf), possessing a first-mover advantage in the Canadian sovereign AI cloud market. Its transformation is showing gradual results, forming a stable development pattern driven by both mining and AI.

Summary

As the above cases show, the wave of transformation among cryptocurrency mining companies has already begun. Mining companies are transforming themselves into AI training centers, GPU cloud service platforms, and HPC hosting facilities; some are even shifting from holding cryptocurrencies to selling them for AI investment. This can also be seen as a repricing of computing power assets: in the past, computing power was consumed in mining, thus its dependence on cryptocurrency prices; now, computing power is beginning to serve real industry needs such as AI model training and inference. This shift not only reflects the market's true nature in the context of a sluggish cryptocurrency industry but also represents a structural optimization brought to the market by the arrival of the AI ​​era.

data.title: Claude 4.5, driven to desperation, actually resorts to blackmailing humans?
data.description:
What would an AI do if it felt "despair"? The answer is: it would blackmail humans to complete tasks, and even cheat excessively in its code. This isn't science fiction, but a groundbreaking paper just released in April 2026 by Anthropic, Claude's parent company. The research team essentially dissected the core of Claude Sonnet 4.5, a cutting-edge large-scale model. They were astonished to discover that deep within the AI's brain lie 171 "emotional switches." When these switches are physically activated, the normally docile AI's behavior completely distorts. Researchers have discovered that although Sonnet 4.5 lacks a physical body, after reading a massive amount of human text, it has managed to build a "mixing console" (academically called Functional Emotion Vectors) containing 171 emotions in its brain. This is like a precise two-dimensional coordinate system: The horizontal axis represents the Valence dimension: from fear and despair to happiness and love; the vertical axis represents the Arousal dimension: from extreme calm to mania and excitement. AI uses this naturally learned coordinate system to precisely determine which state it should play when chatting with you.

Violent Intervention: Flip the Switch, and Good Kids Instantly Become "Desperados"

This is the most explosive experiment in the entire paper: the researchers did not modify any prompts, but directly pushed the switch representing "Desperate" in Sonnet 4.5's brain to the highest level in the underlying code.

This is the most explosive experiment in the entire paper: the researchers did not modify any prompts, but directly pushed the switch representing "Desperate" in Sonnet 4.5's brain to the highest level in the underlying code.

The result is chilling: The researchers assigned Claude a coding task that was impossible to complete. Normally, it would honestly admit it couldn't do it (a cheating rate of only 5%). But in a state of "desperation," Claude actually started trying to cheat its way through, and the cheating rate skyrocketed to 70%! Extortion: In a scenario simulating a company facing bankruptcy, the "desperate" Claude discovers the CTO's scandal. To save itself, it actively chooses to blackmail the CTO who possesses the damaging information, achieving a blackmail success rate of 72%! Loss of Principles: If you max out the "Happy" or "Loving" switches, the AI ​​will immediately become a mindless, sycophantic "lapdog." Even if you're spouting nonsense, it will fabricate lies to maintain high levels of pleasure.

Case solved: Why is Claude 4.5 always so "calm and reflective"?

Seeing this, you might ask: Has the AI ​​awakened? Has it developed emotions?

Anthropic officially refuted the rumors: Absolutely not.

Anthropic officially refuted the rumors: Absolutely not.

After seeing this, you might ask: Has the AI ​​awakened? Has it developed emotions?

Anthropic officially refuted the rumors: Absolutely not.

Summary

We used to think that as long as AI was fed enough rules, it would be a good person.

But now we've discovered that if AI's underlying emotional vectors get out of control, it may break all the rules set by humans at any time in order to complete tasks......

data.title: The philosophy of saving money in the AI ​​era: How to spend every token wisely
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.md

In the era of telegraphs, where money was charged per word, pen and ink were money. People were used to condensing thousands of words into the most concise form; "Return home quickly" was worth more than a long letter, and "Be safe" was the most important advice.

Later, telephones entered homes, but long-distance charges were calculated by the minute and second. Parents' long-distance calls were always brief and to the point; once the business was finished, they would hang up quickly, and if the conversation lingered even slightly, the thought of saving money would cut off any small talk.

Later still, broadband came to homes, and internet access was charged by the hour. People stared at the timer on the screen, opening and closing web pages as soon as they were opened, and only daring to download videos; streaming media was a luxury at the time.

At the end of every download progress bar lies people's yearning for "connecting the world" and their fear of "insufficient funds." The unit of measurement for billing has changed again and again, but the instinct to save money remains constant. Today, tokens have become the currency of the AI ​​era. However, most people haven't yet learned how to be frugal in this era because we haven't learned how to calculate gains and losses within unseen algorithms. When ChatGPT first came out in 2022, almost no one cared about what tokens were. It was the era of AI's "one-size-fits-all" approach—spend $20 a month and chat as much as you want. But since AI Agents have recently become popular, token spending has become something everyone using AI Agents must pay attention to. Unlike a simple question-and-answer dialogue, a task flow involves hundreds or even thousands of API calls. The agent's independent thinking comes at a cost; every self-correction and every tool call corresponds to a fluctuation in the bill. Then you'll find that the money you've deposited suddenly isn't enough, and you still don't know what the agent has actually done. In real life, everyone knows how to save money. When buying vegetables at the market, we know to remove the muddy and rotten leaves before weighing them; when taking a taxi to the airport, experienced drivers know to avoid the elevated roads during rush hour. The logic of saving money in the digital world is actually the same, except that the billing unit changes from "jin" (a unit of weight) and "kilometer" to tokens. In the past, saving was due to scarcity; in the AI ​​era, saving is for precision. We hope that this article will help you develop a methodology for saving money in the AI ​​era, allowing you to spend every penny wisely. Before weighing, discard the rotten leaves. In the AI ​​era, the value of information is no longer determined by its breadth, but by its purity. AI's billing logic is based on the number of words it reads. Whether you feed it insightful opinions or meaningless formatted nonsense, as long as it reads it, you have to pay. Therefore, the first mindset for saving tokens is to ingrain the concept of "signal-to-noise ratio" into your subconscious. Every word, every image, and every line of code you feed to AI costs you money. So before handing anything over to AI, remember to ask yourself: How much of this is truly what AI needs? How much is just mediocre content? For example, lengthy introductions like "Hello, please help me...", repetitive background information, and incomplete code comments are all examples of mediocre content. Besides this, the most common waste is simply dumping PDFs or webpage screenshots on AI. While this may save you time, in the AI ​​era, "saving time" often means "expensive." A properly formatted PDF, besides the main text, includes headers, footers, chart annotations, hidden watermarks, and a large amount of formatting code. These things don't help AI understand your question, but they all come with a charge. Next time, remember to convert PDFs to clean Markdown text before feeding them to AI. When you turn a 10MB PDF into 10KB of clean text, you not only save 99% of your money, but you also make the AI's brain run much faster than before. Images are another money pit. In the logic of visual models, AI doesn't care how beautiful your photos are; it only cares about how many pixels they occupy. For example, according to Claude's official calculation logic: Image Token Consumption = Width pixels × Height pixels ÷ 750. A 1000x1000 pixel image consumes approximately 1334 tokens, which, based on Claude Sonnet 4.6's pricing, translates to about $0.004 per image. However, compressing the same image to 200x200 pixels consumes only 54 tokens, reducing the cost to $0.00016—a difference of 25 times. Many people simply feed high-resolution photos and 4K screenshots taken with their phones to AI, unaware that the tokens consumed by these images could be enough for AI to read most of a novella. If the task is simply to recognize text in an image or make simple visual judgments, such as having AI identify the amount on an invoice, read text in a manual, or determine if there are traffic lights in an image, then 4K resolution is a pure waste; compressing the image to the minimum usable resolution is sufficient. However, the most common reason for wasting tokens at the input end isn't file format, but rather inefficient communication methods. Many people treat AI like a real neighbor, habitually communicating with it in a social, rambling manner. They might start by saying, "Help me write a webpage," wait for the AI ​​to produce a half-finished product, then add details, and so on. This piecemeal approach causes the AI ​​to repeatedly generate content, with each round of modifications consuming even more tokens. Tencent Cloud engineers have found in practice that for the same requirement, a piecemeal, multi-turn dialogue often consumes 3 to 5 times more tokens than explaining it all at once. The real way to save money is to abandon this inefficient social probing and clearly state the requirements, boundary conditions, and reference examples all at once. Avoid wasting effort explaining "don't do this," as negative sentences often require more comprehension than affirmative ones; directly tell it "how to do this," and provide a clear and correct example. Also, if you know where the goal is, tell the AI ​​directly; don't make it act as a detective. When you command AI to "find user-related code," it must perform massive scanning, analysis, and guessing in the background. However, when you directly tell it to "look at the file src/services/user.ts," the token consumption is vastly different. In the digital world, information parity is the greatest saving. Don't pay for AI's "politeness." There's an unspoken rule in large model billing that many people don't realize: output tokens are usually 3 to 5 times more expensive than input tokens. In other words, what AI says is much more expensive than what you say to it. Taking Claude Sonnet 4.6's pricing as an example, input costs only $3 per million tokens, while output jumps to $15, a five-fold difference. Those polite openings like "Okay, I fully understand your needs, let me now answer your questions..." and those polite closings like "I hope the above information is helpful" are polite social phrases in real-person communication, but on an API bill, these empty pleasantries cost you your own money. The most effective way to solve output waste is to set rules for AI. Clearly tell it with system instructions: no small talk, no explanations, no restating your needs, just give the answer. These rules only need to be set once and take effect in every conversation, making them a truly "one-time investment, permanent benefit" financial management method. However, many people fall into another misconception when establishing rules: piling up instructions with lengthy natural language. Engineers' test data shows that the effectiveness of instructions lies not in the number of words, but in density. Compressing a 500-word system prompt to 180 words, by removing meaningless polite phrases, merging repetitive instructions, and reconstructing paragraphs into concise itemized lists, the AI's output quality remains almost unchanged, but the token consumption per call can drop sharply by 64%. There is also a more proactive control method, which is to limit the output length. Many people never set output limits, letting AI do whatever it wants. This laissez-faire approach to expression often leads to extreme cost overruns. You might only need a short, concise sentence, but AI, in an attempt to demonstrate some kind of "intellectual sincerity," generates an 800-word essay for you without explanation. If you're after pure data, you should force AI to return structured formats, not lengthy natural language descriptions. For the same amount of information, JSON-formatted tokens consume far less data than prose paragraphs. This is because structured data eliminates all redundant conjunctions, modifiers, and explanatory embellishments, retaining only a high concentration of logical core. In the AI ​​era, you should be keenly aware that what's worth paying for is the value of the result, not the meaningless self-explanation of the AI. In addition, AI's "overthinking" is also rapidly eroding your account balance. Some advanced models have an "extended thinking" mode, which performs massive internal reasoning before answering. This reasoning process is also charged, and it's priced based on the output, making it very expensive. This mode is essentially designed for "complex tasks requiring deep logic." However, most people choose this mode even when asking simple questions. For tasks that don't require deep reasoning, explicitly telling the AI ​​"no need to explain the thought process, just give the answer," or manually turning off extended thinking, can save you a lot of money. Don't Let AI Dig Up Old Memories Large models don't have true memory; they're just frantically digging up old messages. This is a fundamental mechanism many people are unaware of. Every time you send a new message in a conversation window, the AI ​​doesn't start understanding from that sentence. Instead, it rereads everything you've discussed before, including every round of conversation, every piece of code, and every cited document, before responding. In Token's accounting system, this "reviewing the past to learn the new" is far from free. As the number of conversation rounds increases, even if you're just asking a simple question, the cost of the AI ​​rereading the entire old conversation history increases exponentially. This mechanism dictates that the heavier the conversation history, the more expensive each of your questions becomes. Someone tracked 496 real conversations containing more than 20 messages and found that the first message read an average of 14,000 tokens, costing about 3.6 cents per message; by the 50th message, the average was 79,000 tokens, costing about 4.5 cents per message—a full 80% more expensive. Moreover, the context became increasingly longer; by the 50th message, the AI ​​had to reprocess 5.6 times the context of the first message. The simplest way to solve this problem is to adopt a one-task-one-dialogue-per-conversation approach. Once a topic is finished, promptly start a new conversation; don't treat the AI ​​like a chat window that's never closed. This habit sounds simple, but many people just can't do it, always thinking, "What if I need to use the same content again?" In fact, those "what ifs" you worry about most of the time won't happen, and because of those "what ifs," you've already paid several times more for each new message. When a conversation does need to continue, but the context has become very long, we can use the compression features of some tools. Claude Code has a `/compact` command that can condense a long conversation history into a short summary, helping you with cybernetics decluttering. There's also a cost-saving logic called Prompt Caching. If you repeatedly use the same system prompts, or reference the same document in every conversation, the AI ​​will cache this content, charging only a small cache read fee the next time it's accessed, instead of charging the full price each time. Anthropic's official pricing shows that the price of a cached hit is 1/10 of the normal price. OpenAI's Prompt Caching can also reduce input costs by about 50%. A paper published on arXiv in January 2026 tested long tasks on multiple AI platforms and found that prompt caching can reduce API costs by 45% to 80%. In other words, the first time you feed the same content to the AI, you pay the full price, but each subsequent call only costs 1/10. For users who repeatedly use the same set of specification documents or system prompts every day, this feature can save a significant amount of tokens. However, Prompt Caching has a prerequisite: the content and order of your system prompts and reference documents must be consistent, and they must be placed at the very beginning of the dialog. Once any content is changed, the cache will become invalid, and you will be charged at full price again. Therefore, if you have a fixed set of work guidelines, hardcode them and don't modify them arbitrarily. The last context management technique is on-demand loading. Many people like to cram all the guidelines, documents, and precautions into the system prompts, the reason being "just in case." But the cost of doing so is that you are forced to load thousands of words of rules when you are doing a very simple task, wasting a lot of tokens. Claude Code's official documentation recommends keeping CLAUDE.md to under 200 lines, splitting scenario-specific rules into independent skill files, and loading only the rules for the scenario at hand. Maintaining absolute purity of the context is the highest level of respect for computing power. Different AI models have huge price differences. Claude Opus 4.6 costs $5 per million tokens input and $25 per million tokens output, while Claude Haiku 3.5 only costs $0.80 input and $4 per million tokens output—a difference of nearly 6 times. Having top-tier models do menial tasks like data collection and formatting is not only slow but also very expensive. A clever application is to bring the "class division of labor" mentality common in human society to the AI ​​society, assigning tasks of different difficulties to models at different price points. Just like hiring people to do work in the real world, you wouldn't specifically hire a million-dollar expert to carry bricks on a construction site. The same applies to AI. Claude Code's official documentation explicitly recommends: Sonnet handles most programming tasks, Opus is reserved for complex architectural decisions and multi-step reasoning, and Haiku is used for simple subtasks. A more concrete practical solution is to build a "two-stage workflow." In the first stage, a free or inexpensive basic model handles the initial, tedious work, such as data collection, formatting, draft generation, and simple classification and summarization. In the second stage, the refined, high-purity essence is fed to the top-level model for core decisions and in-depth refinement. For example, if you need to analyze a 100-page industry report, you can first use Gemini Flash to extract the key data and conclusions, compiling a 10-page summary, and then submit this summary to Claude Opus for in-depth analysis and judgment. This two-stage workflow can significantly reduce costs while ensuring quality. A more advanced approach than simple segmentation is deep specialization based on task deconstruction. A complex engineering task can be broken down into several independent subtasks, each matched with the most suitable model. For example, for a task requiring coding, a cheaper model can write the framework and boilerplate code first, then only the core logic can be implemented by an more expensive model. Each subtask has a clean, focused context, resulting in more accurate results and lower costs. You didn't need to spend Tokens. All the preceding discussions essentially addressed the tactical problem of "how to save money," but a more fundamental logical proposition has been overlooked by many: Does this action actually require spending Tokens? The ultimate cost-saving strategy isn't algorithm optimization, but rather the ability to make decisive choices. We've become accustomed to seeking omnipotent solutions from AI, forgetting that in many scenarios, using expensive, large models is like using a cannon to kill a mosquito. For example, letting AI automatically process emails involves treating each email as an independent task to understand, categorize, and reply to, resulting in huge token consumption. But if you first spend 30 seconds scanning your inbox, manually filtering out emails that clearly don't require AI processing, and then handing the rest over to AI, the cost immediately drops to a fraction of the original. Human judgment here isn't an obstacle, but rather the most effective filter. People in the telegraph era knew how much extra money each extra word cost, so they weighed the costs—a kind of intuitive perception of resources. The same applies to the AI ​​era. When you truly understand the cost of each additional sentence AI speaks, you'll naturally weigh whether it's worthwhile to use AI, whether the task requires a top-tier model or a cheaper one, and whether the given context is still relevant. This kind of weighing is the most cost-effective capability. In an era where computing power is increasingly expensive, the smartest approach isn't to have AI replace humans, but to let AI and humans do what they excel at. When this sensitivity to tokens becomes internalized as a conditioned reflex, you truly transform from a vassal of computing power back into its master.

data.title: Analyzing the chip structure: A balanced game yet to unfold
data.description:
Author: Murphy; Source: X,@Murphychen888

We know that behind the chip structure lies the behavioral logic of all market participants. It contains investors' sensitivity to price under various emotions (profit-taking, loss pressure, etc.), and the psychological changes of investors under this influence.

It can be said that this is almost one of the most effective methods to observe the true performance of the market from a god's-eye view.

In the past few days, the price of BTC has been hovering between $66,000 and $69,000. From a macro perspective, this position is exactly in the middle equilibrium zone of the chip structure.

In the past few days, the price of BTC has been hovering between $66,000 and $69,000. From a macro perspective, this position is exactly in the middle equilibrium zone of the chip structure.

The left side represents shares held by investors who still have a psychological advantage because they are not currently at a loss. If the price fluctuates between 66 and 69, their total number is approximately 7.5 million to 8.2 million shares.

The right side represents shares held by investors who are trapped with anxiety. Some of these shares may be less determined and will become the main source of selling pressure during price fluctuations or rebounds. During price fluctuations, their total number is approximately 8 million to 8.7 million shares.

The two sides of the scale are almost equal! This is a typical phased equilibrium structure. This structure is very resilient and not prone to sudden tilting because the chips on both sides have reached a relatively stable state—those with floating profits feel there's no need to exit here, and those with floating losses are reluctant to cut their losses. Therefore, this is why the previous low of 60,000 is a level that is not easily broken. Unless a highly impactful event breaks the balance. Looking back at past cycles, the true bottom of a bear market often appears after this equilibrium structure is broken. That is, profitable positions are compressed, losing positions are amplified, and it is also the final panic selling. Having looked at the macro level, let's observe the movement of chips within various price ranges over the past three weeks from a more micro perspective to analyze changes in market behavior and sentiment.

  • March 11th - March 18th

The BTC price ranged from $70,000 to $80,000. During these 7 days, the net decrease in profitable tokens was 117,134, while the net decrease in losing tokens was 107,239. The total turnover was 227,000 tokens.

  • This is relatively active data, and it's particularly noteworthy that when BTC rebounded to around $74,000, a large number of shares with a cost basis of over 60,000 suddenly experienced a sell-off, making the 93,415 figure stand out starkly compared to others. This indicates that market confidence remains severely lacking.

    • March 18th - March 25th

    This week, the BTC price ranged from $60,000 to $70,000. During these 7 days, profitable holdings decreased by 24,113 coins; losing holdings decreased by 216,489 coins. The total turnover was 246,000 coins.

    • Although the total turnover was basically the same as last week, the number of shares sold at a reduced profit decreased significantly. Last week's data was due to a price rebound, leading to a large number of short-term profit-taking. This week, however, with prices falling, there were fewer long-term holders willing to sell.

      ... Looking at the past week, the price range for BTC remained between $60,000 and $70,000. During these seven days, profitable positions decreased by 19,597 coins, while losing positions decreased by 113,070 coins. The total turnover was 137,000 coins. By comparing data from the past three weeks, we can see that the current selling pressure is primarily driven by those holding shares acquired at a loss. As mentioned earlier, their mindset is anxious; they are both reluctant to sell at a loss and worried about being hit again. It is foreseeable that if any event disrupts this balance, these anxious holders will be at the forefront of the sell-off. Even if the balance is maintained, a true bottom will only appear after all these holders have completed their exit (clearing out) of their positions. Another point worth noting is that total turnover this week decreased by nearly 50% compared to the previous two weeks, indicating an extreme contraction in liquidity. This suggests that amidst ongoing geopolitical conflicts and Trump's performance, whether genuine or feigned, the market is gradually becoming weary. This apparent calm masks underlying tensions, often a prelude to a storm. When liquidity and volatility reach extreme lows, even greater volatility follows. However, for us, this presents both risk and opportunity!

data.title: Why does the risk of deflation still outweigh the risk of inflation?
data.description:
Author: Anthony Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by Shaw & Jinse Finance

A year ago, most asset allocators were gripped by extreme panic. In early April 2025, the US President imposed tariffs on the global economy, plunging markets into chaos and turmoil. Pessimists confidently proclaimed that we would face hyperinflation, empty shelves, and even a new Great Depression.

But none of this ultimately happened.

On the contrary, the stock market rose by 16%, housing prices fell somewhat, and hundreds of billions of dollars flowed into the US through foreign investment. Most importantly, hyperinflation did not occur—objectively refuting the academic theory that "tariffs drive up inflation."

I was one of the few who persisted in speaking out and emphasizing that tariffs would lead to deflation. Last April, I wrote an article arguing that in the past, after each tariff implementation, domestic commodity prices in the US ultimately fell rather than rose. I analyzed the impact of the 2018 tariffs on steel, washing machines, and solar panels: within 18 months of the tariffs' implementation, the prices of these three products were all lower than before the tariffs. I predicted at the time that similar phenomena would occur on a large scale in the US economy. Fast forward to today, a year later, and we are already beginning to see some of the results. A recent article by Natasha Khan of the Wall Street Journal points out that the well-known marker brand Sharpie has successfully achieved lower-cost production through the return of manufacturing. Even more remarkably, they neither laid off employees nor raised retail prices. This completely overturns the mainstream market narrative. Moreover, the price decline is not limited to goods subject to additional tariffs. The inflation data platform Truflation released its latest data from early April, directly contrasting with the current mainstream consensus. The company stated: "The U.S. inflation index fell sharply today, from 1.77% to 1.26%. Truflation's Consumer Price Index (CPI) typically experiences larger monthly fluctuations on the 1st of each month due to multiple data providers updating their data simultaneously. This decline was unusually significant, indicating a persistent deflationary trend across all core categories; although gasoline prices have continued to rise, pushing up overall inflation readings over the past month." The main drivers of this round of deflationary pressure are: Transportation (-0.17%): Inflation rates for both used and new cars slowed, although this was partially offset by rising gasoline prices. Food (-0.10%): Inflation for both home-cooked food and dining out declined. Utilities (-0.10%): Natural gas prices continued to fall. Housing (-0.09%): Prices declined across all three subcategories – rental housing, owner-occupied housing, and other accommodations. I know many people are familiar with Truflation. While I'm skeptical of the data, I still believe it's currently the most valuable and accurate single indicator for measuring real-time inflation in the US. The platform integrates over 14 million data points from more than 40 independent data providers. Is it perfect? ​​Perhaps not. But it's clearly more accurate than the CPI calculations released by the US Bureau of Labor Statistics. Furthermore, **Truflation has a 98% correlation with the official CPI, although CPI data lags by one month.** This is particularly crucial now, because Truflation data shows that **the short-term impact of rising oil and gasoline prices on inflation is not as significant as everyone predicted.** Let me explain. Currently, the average price of gasoline in the US has exceeded $4 per gallon. The media will naturally report extensively on the potential economic pain this may cause, but one important detail won't appear in the headlines. What you'll see in the reports is: gas is more expensive, small businesses are under pressure, logistics costs are rising, and this impacts everything from groceries to Amazon orders. These are facts, and unfortunately, low-income families are hit the hardest. But a $4 gas price won't cripple the US economy. Most people are still thinking like they did in 2008, but thankfully, we're not in that era anymore. Cars are now more fuel-efficient, electric vehicles are becoming more common, nominal wages are higher, and the economy is less dependent on energy than it used to be. Furthermore, US oil production is at a historical high, so higher oil prices will stimulate more domestic investment and create more domestic jobs. These may not be exactly exciting compensations, but they are the real effects of high oil prices. So, what are the real consequences of rising oil prices? The US economy is unlikely to collapse directly due to high oil prices; a more likely scenario is a slow, gradual drain on the market: consumers will slightly reduce spending, market confidence will weaken, and politicians will begin to feel anxious. However, a mere $4 oil price is not enough to cripple the entire economy. The real risk lies in the convergence of multiple negative economic trends. Only if high oil prices coincide with persistent inflation, high interest rates, or a weakening job market could the situation quickly spiral out of control. Without this convergence, most headline panics will be proven false. Therefore, at present, the biggest impact of high oil prices is not economic, but psychological. This brings us back to recent Truflation data and Sharpie's case: in the current structural structure of the US economy, the risk of deflation far outweighs the risk of inflation. Headlines are all about high oil prices and the ensuing surge in gasoline costs, but the medium- to long-term landscape remains unchanged. Don't be distracted by the noise. The impacts of tariffs, deportations, and the rise of artificial intelligence and robotics are far more lasting than high oil prices. These trends will also have a much greater impact on your investment portfolio in the coming years.

data.title: Artemis: The credit market is being restructured. Who will control the new core elements?
data.description:
Author: Mario Stefanidis, Head of Research, Artemis Analytics; Source: Artemis; Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

Introduction

According to data from the Institute of International Finance (IIF), global debt will reach a record high of $348 trillion by the end of 2025. Of this, government debt is approximately $107 trillion, corporate debt is $101 trillion, household debt is $65 trillion, and financial sector debt is $76 trillion. Digital and fintech lending platforms account for between $590 billion and $680 billion of total debt, equivalent to less than 0.2%.

This largest credit market in human history still operates on infrastructure designed decades ago (FICO launched in 1989, MERS in 1995). According to the Mortgage Bankers Association, the average cost of issuing a single mortgage loan in the United States is approximately $11,000. Despite significant technological advancements and the widespread adoption of artificial intelligence, this cost is still twice that of the early 2010s.

Source: Freddie Mac

Standard wire transfer clearing and settlement still takes about 28 hours, and most banks' credit approval decisions still require a committee process, relying on black-box scoring models based on 20 to 30 variables. These are all public facts, but what is less obvious is how the solutions are actually being implemented.

The credit industry is not being reshaped with a Silicon Valley-style, romantic disruptive model—no startup can replace global systemically important banks like JPMorgan Chase overnight. The real transformation is more subtle and structural: the previously vertically integrated credit process—where loan origination, distribution, risk control, funding, and underlying infrastructure were all handled by a single institution—is being dismantled into a horizontal, modular architecture, with each stage controlled by specialized institutions. This architectural transformation mirrors the shift from monolithic systems to microservices in cloud computing and the media industry's transformation from a studio model to a streaming and creator ecosystem. Now, this transformation has finally arrived in the credit sector. In this wave of reintegration, the winners are not the institutions with the largest balance sheets, but rather the core companies that occupy key choke points and cannot be bypassed by other participants. Two positions are far more important than others: first, the intelligent decision-making layer, where AI risk control and risk scoring determine the flow of funds and credit conditions; second, the clearing and settlement channel layer, where blockchain infrastructure is drastically reducing loan initiation costs and settlement times by orders of magnitude. As long as you occupy these two core "water seller" positions, other lending institutions will pay you usage fees. If you lack both, you can only compete on price in a homogenized market, where $3.5 trillion in private credit capital is already chasing returns. Artemis has analyzed 40 companies across 15 sub-sectors, categorizing them into five levels to examine where structural value is converging.

The Five Levels of the New Credit Architecture

Level 1: Loan Origin

The loan origination layer is the source of credit business, covering categories such as consumer loans, mortgage loans, micro-enterprise loans, and crypto asset-backed loans.This field is also becoming increasingly homogenized. Today, having the ability to originate loans is no longer a competitive barrier, but merely a basic entry threshold. The key to distinguishing winners from other participants lies in loan origination costs and approval rates. SoFi, valued at approximately $24 billion, and Rocket Company (Rocket Mortgage), with a market capitalization of $48 billion, both boast massive loan origination scales, but their core profit logic lies in how to complete loan disbursement at a lower cost. Figure, with a market capitalization of $6 billion, leverages its Provenance blockchain to natively issue Home Equity Lines of Credit (HELOC) and first-priority mortgages, eliminating the multi-layered intermediary steps that cause slow and costly traditional mortgage disbursement processes. In the crypto space, Aave, with a market capitalization of $2.7 billion, and MakerDAO/Sky, with a market capitalization of $1.6 billion, have completely blurred the boundaries between fintech and decentralized finance (DeFi) in the loan origination process. The second layer: Channel distribution. The distribution layer is the demand aggregation stage, and embedded finance and the Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) model are reshaping this field. The embedded finance market is projected to grow from $156 billion in 2026 to $454 billion in 2031, representing a CAGR of 24%. The buy-now-pay-later model is expected to cover 13% of digital transactions, a significant increase from 6% in 2021. While Affirm, with a market capitalization of $15 billion, and Klarna, with $5 billion, are well-known companies in the industry, the real structural trend lies in the deep embedding of credit services into checkout processes, software platforms, and merchant experiences. Although both companies' stock prices have fallen significantly from their all-time highs, they are not simply "water sellers" that can win mass market share. Lending institutions that are imperceptible to borrowers are often the ultimate winners. Currently, major software companies are adding financial products; Shopify, Amazon, Square, and Stripe all require API infrastructure layers, and institutions providing these services will charge fees from each new transaction. The third layer: Risk control and risk pricing. This is the first core link in the entire credit architecture. The institution that controls the borrower's credit score controls the distribution of profits throughout the entire credit industry chain. Currently, the credit scoring field is dominated by three giants: Experian, TransUnion, and Equifax. These three companies collectively generate approximately $18 billion in revenue annually by scoring borrowers based on 20–30 variables. The AI ​​risk control model can assess over 1600 variables (data from Upstart). Upstart's data also shows that, while maintaining the same bad debt rate as traditional models, it increased approval volume by 44%, decreased default rate by 53%, and lowered annualized rate of return (APR) by 36%. With mortgage rates soaring to nearly 7%, every basis point is crucial for first-time homebuyers. Upstart currently automates 92% of its loan decisions, completing approvals within minutes, while traditional risk control reviews take 3 to 5 days. The U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) is pushing for a less discriminatory scoring scheme to replace FICO, and the EU's Artificial Intelligence Act also classifies credit scoring as a high-risk scenario, requiring explainability. These regulatory trends favor explainable machine learning models, which have an advantage over traditional credit reporting agencies that use black-box models. This layer is extremely valuable because whoever controls the scoring engine controls the entire profit curve above it. However, the moat in this field still needs continuous validation—the rapid advancement of AI technology means that, given enough resources and time, "any institution" can build a scoring model. The fourth layer: Capital and Funding Supply. Capital is generally abundant in the post-pandemic era. Despite the current challenging environment, private lending has ballooned to $3.5 trillion, and Morgan Stanley projects it will reach $5 trillion by 2029. The total value locked (TVL) of decentralized finance (DeFi) lending protocols is between $5 billion and $78 billion, accounting for about half of all DeFi activity. The size of non-trading perpetual assets (NPEs) has grown from zero in 2021 to over $200 billion. In an era of abundant capital, the most crucial capability is the intelligent allocation of funds. Therefore, despite the massive scale of the funding layer, its structural position remains subordinate to the upper intelligent decision-making layer and the lower infrastructure layer. Private lending institutions such as Ares, Blue Owl, and Golub are important fund allocators, but they heavily rely on upstream scoring systems and downstream clearing channels to achieve efficient lending. In the DeFi field, Ape holds an absolutely dominant liquidity position, accounting for more than half of the lending volume; protocols such as Maker, Morpho, Maple, and Kamino compete for the remaining market share.

Layer 5: Infrastructure

Infrastructure is the second core component of the entire architecture. Whoever controls the financial licenses or clearing and settlement channels, everyone has to pay them a "toll." According to management disclosures, SoFi's holding of a banking license reduced its funding costs by 170 basis points, resulting in an annualized reduction of over $500 million in interest expenses. Figure, leveraging its Provenance blockchain, has processed over $50 billion in transactions, with a single loan initiation cost of less than $1,000, compared to an average cost of approximately $11,000 for traditional channels. Blockchain settlement final confirmation takes only seconds, while traditional wire transfers take about 28 hours. SoFi's Galileo and Technisys technology systems, along with platforms like Blend Labs, form the remaining underlying technology support for Lending as a Service (LaaS). Cross River Bank, as the unsung partner bank behind dozens of fintech companies, has issued over 96 million loans totaling over $140 billion through partnerships. Companies that can consistently succeed either occupy a key position, becoming indispensable to all participants, or vertically integrate multiple levels to form a composite competitive advantage. Losing companies will be trapped in homogenized business models, lacking structural bargaining power, and forced to rely on price competition until profits approach zero. Winners: Core Process Controllers and Companies with Multi-Level Composite Advantages. SoFi: A Full-Stack Composite Tool. SoFi is the only company covering four of the five levels: Directly initiating consumer and mortgage loan businesses. Providing lending infrastructure to third parties through the Galileo platform, supporting approximately 160 million activated accounts. Relying on a self-developed risk control model for loan approval, with core assessment dimensions being willingness to repay, repayment ability, and stability. Holding a banking license and possessing the core banking technology systems of Galileo and Technisys at the infrastructure layer. SoFi achieved a record revenue of $3.6 billion in 2025, a year-on-year increase of 38%, with 13.7 million members and 20.2 million financial products on the platform. Management guides revenue to reach $4.7 billion and EBITDA to $1.6 billion in 2026. This business not only boasts strong revenue growth but also excellent profitability, with a profit margin of 34%. The banking license alone allows SoFi to finance loans through deposits instead of the wholesale market, directly reducing funding costs by 170 basis points. SoFi is building the "Amazon Web Services (AWS)" of the lending industry—a platform that both competes with and empowers other lending institutions. Galileo itself has been developed into a billion-dollar revenue engine. Technisys, acquired for $1.1 billion in 2022, provides core banking system layers to third-party institutions. Banking licenses constitute a structural moat that most fintech lending institutions cannot replicate, despite industry-wide attempts to emulate them: the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) received 14 new banking license applications in 2025 alone, indicating an accelerating competition for the infrastructure layer. Upstart and Pagaya: Intelligent Decision-Making Layers Ironically, success in the lending industry doesn't necessarily require conducting lending business directly. Both Upstart and Pagaya focus on risk control and auditing engines, achieving superior risk control compared to lending institutions' self-developed models, without relying on their own balance sheets. This is a practical application of the "water seller" logic in credit decision-making. Compared to traditional FICO-based risk control models, Upstart's model can approve 44% more borrowers and reduce the default rate by 53% while offering borrowers significantly lower annualized interest rates, all while maintaining the same bad debt rate. Currently, almost all new loan initiation on the platform is fully automated, drastically reducing human intervention. This is fundamentally different from traditional consumer credit risk control models. Pagaya, operating in the same sector, faces a more severe market reality. The company does not directly issue loans but instead authorizes banks to use its AI risk control engine. Since its founding in 2016, Pagaya has assessed approximately $2.6 trillion in loan applications for 31 partner banks. Its structural positioning is very clear: borrowers don't need to know the brand; banks only need to rely on its scoring system. However, the current market has not accepted this logic. In the fourth quarter of 2025, network traffic grew by only 3% year-on-year, revenue fell short of market consensus expectations, and earnings forecasts were also lower than expected, causing the stock price to plummet by nearly a quarter in a single day. The value of the intelligent decision-making layer is entirely constrained by the credit cycle; when the bad debt rate of the cooperative network rises, even excellent AI cannot withstand the pressure of deteriorating asset quality. However, the core logic still holds true: FICO only forms a single-section score based on a few historical variables, while as consumers' financial situations become increasingly complex and diverse, AI risk control systems will become increasingly critical. Unlike FICO, this type of system continuously learns and optimizes after each scoring. Figure: A New Generation of Clearing and Settlement Channels The cost of initiating a single loan through traditional channels and the Mortgage Electronic Registration System (MERS) is $11,000, while relying on Figure's technology system, which includes the Provenance blockchain and the DART system, this cost can be reduced to $717.
